question: B-3. Prove that there are exactly three right-angled triangles whose sides are integers while the area is numerically equal to twice the perimeter.
solution:
B-3. All Pythagorean triples can be obtained from \( x=\lambda\left(p^{2}-q^{2}\right), y=2 \lambda p q \), \( z=\lambda\left(p^{2}+q^{2}\right) \) where \( 0<q<p,(p, q)=1 \) and \( p \not \equiv q \bmod 2, \lambda \) being any natural number.

The problem requires that \( \frac{1}{2} x y=2(x+y+z) \). This condition can be written \( \lambda^{2}\left(p^{2}-q^{2}\right)(p q)=2 \lambda\left(p^{2}-q^{2}+2 p q+p^{2}+q^{2}\right) \) or simply \( \lambda(p-q) q=4 \). Since \( p-q \) is odd it follows that \( p-q=1 \) and the only possibilities for \( q \) are \( 1,2,4 \).
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\text { If } q=1, p=2, \lambda=4, x=12, y=16, z=20 \text {. } \\
\text { If } q=2, p=3, \lambda=2, x=10, y=24, z=26 . \\
\text { If } q=4, p=5, \lambda=1, x=9, y=40, z=41 .
\end{array}
\]

descriptive_long.question: B-3. Prove that there are exactly three right-angled triangles whose sides are integers while the area is numerically equal to twice the perimeter.
descriptive_long.solution:
B-3. All Pythagorean triples can be obtained from \( sidelengthone=scalefactor\left(parameterp^{2}-parameterq^{2}\right), sidelengthtwo=2 scalefactor parameterp parameterq \), \( sidelengththree=scalefactor\left(parameterp^{2}+parameterq^{2}\right) \) where \( 0<parameterq<parameterp,(parameterp, parameterq)=1 \) and \( parameterp \not \equiv parameterq \bmod 2, scalefactor \) being any natural number.

The problem requires that \( \frac{1}{2} sidelengthone sidelengthtwo=2(sidelengthone+sidelengthtwo+sidelengththree) \). This condition can be written \( scalefactor^{2}\left(parameterp^{2}-parameterq^{2}\right)(parameterp parameterq)=2 scalefactor\left(parameterp^{2}-parameterq^{2}+2 parameterp parameterq+parameterp^{2}+parameterq^{2}\right) \) or simply \( scalefactor(parameterp-parameterq) parameterq=4 \). Since \( parameterp-parameterq \) is odd it follows that \( parameterp-parameterq=1 \) and the only possibilities for \( parameterq \) are \( 1,2,4 \).
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\text { If } parameterq=1, parameterp=2, scalefactor=4, sidelengthone=12, sidelengthtwo=16, sidelengththree=20 \text {. } \\
\text { If } parameterq=2, parameterp=3, scalefactor=2, sidelengthone=10, sidelengthtwo=24, sidelengththree=26 . \\
\text { If } parameterq=4, parameterp=5, scalefactor=1, sidelengthone=9, sidelengthtwo=40, sidelengththree=41 .
\end{array}
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question: B-3. Prove that there are exactly three right-angled triangles whose sides are integers while the area is numerically equal to twice the perimeter.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
B-3. All Pythagorean triples can be obtained from \( willowtree=buttercup\left(glassmaker^{2}-driftwood^{2}\right), parchment=2 buttercup glassmaker driftwood \), \( chimneyfire=buttercup\left(glassmaker^{2}+driftwood^{2}\right) \) where \( 0<driftwood<glassmaker,(glassmaker, driftwood)=1 \) and \( glassmaker \not \equiv driftwood \bmod 2, buttercup \) being any natural number.

The problem requires that \( \frac{1}{2} willowtree parchment=2(willowtree+parchment+chimneyfire) \). This condition can be written \( buttercup^{2}\left(glassmaker^{2}-driftwood^{2}\right)(glassmaker driftwood)=2 buttercup\left(glassmaker^{2}-driftwood^{2}+2 glassmaker driftwood+glassmaker^{2}+driftwood^{2}\right) \) or simply \( buttercup(glassmaker-driftwood) driftwood=4 \). Since \( glassmaker-driftwood \) is odd it follows that \( glassmaker-driftwood=1 \) and the only possibilities for \( driftwood \) are \( 1,2,4 \).
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\text { If } driftwood=1, glassmaker=2, buttercup=4, willowtree=12, parchment=16, chimneyfire=20 \text {. } \\
\text { If } driftwood=2, glassmaker=3, buttercup=2, willowtree=10, parchment=24, chimneyfire=26 . \\
\text { If } driftwood=4, glassmaker=5, buttercup=1, willowtree=9, parchment=40, chimneyfire=41 .
\end{array}
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question: B-3. Prove that there are exactly three right-angled triangles whose sides are integers while the area is numerically equal to twice the perimeter.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
B-3. All Pythagorean triples can be obtained from \( midpointlen=shrinkage\left(negativeval^{2}-irrational^{2}\right), interiorlen=2 shrinkage negativeval irrational \), \( centerline=shrinkage\left(negativeval^{2}+irrational^{2}\right) \) where \( 0<irrational<negativeval,(negativeval, irrational)=1 \) and \( negativeval \not \equiv irrational \bmod 2, shrinkage \) being any natural number.

The problem requires that \( \frac{1}{2} midpointlen interiorlen=2(midpointlen+interiorlen+centerline) \). This condition can be written \( shrinkage^{2}\left(negativeval^{2}-irrational^{2}\right)(negativeval irrational)=2 shrinkage\left(negativeval^{2}-irrational^{2}+2 negativeval irrational+negativeval^{2}+irrational^{2}\right) \) or simply \( shrinkage(negativeval-irrational) irrational=4 \). Since \( negativeval-irrational \) is odd it follows that \( negativeval-irrational=1 \) and the only possibilities for \( irrational \) are \( 1,2,4 \).
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\text { If } irrational=1, negativeval=2, shrinkage=4, midpointlen=12, interiorlen=16, centerline=20 \text {. } \\
\text { If } irrational=2, negativeval=3, shrinkage=2, midpointlen=10, interiorlen=24, centerline=26 . \\
\text { If } irrational=4, negativeval=5, shrinkage=1, midpointlen=9, interiorlen=40, centerline=41 .
\end{array}
\]

garbled_string.question: B-3. Prove that there are exactly three right-angled triangles whose sides are integers while the area is numerically equal to twice the perimeter.
garbled_string.solution:
B-3. All Pythagorean triples can be obtained from \( qzxwvtnp=poiulkjh\left(asdflkji^{2}-wertyuio^{2}\right), hjgrksla=2 \, poiulkjh \, asdflkji \, wertyuio \), \( mnbvcxza=poiulkjh\left(asdflkji^{2}+wertyuio^{2}\right) \) where \( 0<wertyuio<asdflkji,(asdflkji, wertyuio)=1 \) and \( asdflkji \not \equiv wertyuio \bmod 2, poiulkjh \) being any natural number.

The problem requires that \( \frac{1}{2} qzxwvtnp \, hjgrksla = 2(qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla+mnbvcxza) \). This condition can be written \( poiulkjh^{2}\left(asdflkji^{2}-wertyuio^{2}\right)(asdflkji \, wertyuio)=2 \, poiulkjh\left(asdflkji^{2}-wertyuio^{2}+2 \, asdflkji \, wertyuio+asdflkji^{2}+wertyuio^{2}\right) \) or simply \( poiulkjh(asdflkji-wertyuio) \, wertyuio=4 \). Since \( asdflkji-wertyuio \) is odd it follows that \( asdflkji-wertyuio=1 \) and the only possibilities for \( wertyuio \) are \( 1,2,4 \).
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\text { If } wertyuio=1, asdflkji=2, poiulkjh=4, qzxwvtnp=12, hjgrksla=16, mnbvcxza=20 \text {. } \\
\text { If } wertyuio=2, asdflkji=3, poiulkjh=2, qzxwvtnp=10, hjgrksla=24, mnbvcxza=26 . \\
\text { If } wertyuio=4, asdflkji=5, poiulkjh=1, qzxwvtnp=9, hjgrksla=40, mnbvcxza=41 .
\end{array}
\]

kernel_variant.question: Prove that there are exactly fifteen right-angled triangles whose side-lengths are positive integers and whose area is numerically equal to twenty-four times their perimeter.  Moreover, decide which of these fifteen triples are primitive, i.e. whose three side-lengths are relatively prime.
kernel_variant.solution:
Notation.  
* Throughout we write (a , b , c) for the (unordered) triple ``leg, leg, hypotenuse'' and agree to display the two legs in non-decreasing order (shorter first).  
* (x , y , z) stands for an arbitrary positive integral solution of  

        \frac{1}{2} xy  = 24 (x + y + z),  x^2 + y^2 = z^2.                (\star )


Step 1.  General parametrisation of all Pythagorean triples  
Every integer right-angled triangle can be written

        x = \lambda ( p^2 - q^2 ), y = 2\lambda pq, z = \lambda ( p^2 + q^2 ),    (1)

where \lambda  \in  \mathbb{N}, 0 < q < p, gcd(p , q) = 1, and p \not\equiv  q (mod 2) (opposite parity).

Step 2.  Translating  ``area = 24 \times  perimeter''  
Area: A = \frac{1}{2}xy = \frac{1}{2}\cdot \lambda ^2(p^2 - q^2)\cdot 2pq = \lambda ^2 pq (p^2 - q^2).  
Perimeter: P = x + y + z = \lambda [(p^2 - q^2) + 2pq + (p^2 + q^2)] = 2\lambda p(p + q).

The condition A = 24 P therefore reads  

        \lambda ^2 pq (p^2 - q^2) = 24\cdot 2\lambda  p (p + q)  
\Leftrightarrow       \lambda  q (p^2 - q^2)   = 48 (p + q).                                            (2)

Since p^2 - q^2 = (p - q)(p + q) and p + q > 0 we may cancel p + q to obtain the core Diophantine relation  

        \lambda  q (p - q) = 48.                                                      (3)

Step 3.  First arithmetical consequences  
(i)  p - q is positive and odd (because p and q have opposite parity).  
(ii) gcd(q , p - q) = 1 (any common divisor would divide both p and q).  

Hence p - q must be an odd divisor of 48, whence  

        p - q \in  {1, 3}.                                                       (4)

Step 4.  Exhaustive analysis of (3)

CASE A.  p - q = 1.  
Equation (3) becomes \lambda  q = 48.  Because gcd(q , 1) = 1, every divisor q of 48 is allowed:

        q \in  {1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 16, 24, 48},  \lambda  = 48 / q.     (5)

Put p = q + 1.  Substituting p and \lambda  in (1) gives

        a_0 = \lambda (2q + 1), b_0 = 2\lambda  q(q + 1), c   = \lambda (2q^2 + 2q + 1).     (6)

(The sub-script 0 reminds us that these are ``raw'' legs; in the final table we swap them whenever b_0 < a_0, although this never happens in Case A.)

CASE B.  p - q = 3.  
Now (3) reads \lambda  q = 48 / 3 = 16.  
Because gcd(q , 3) = 1, q must be a divisor of 16:

        q \in  {1, 2, 4, 8, 16},  \lambda  = 16 / q.                     (7)

Put p = q + 3; inserting in (1) yields

        a_0 = \lambda \cdot 3(2q + 3) = \lambda (6q + 9),  
        b_0 = 2\lambda  q(q + 3),  
        c   = \lambda (2q^2 + 6q + 9).                                    (8)

Here b_0 can occasionally be smaller than a_0, so a final ordering step will be required.

No other values of p - q are possible by (4), so (6) together with (8) already parametrize all triangles whose area equals 24 times their perimeter.

Step 5.  Explicit enumeration  
For each admissible q we compute (a_0 , b_0 , c) and finally write (a , b , c) with a \leq  b.

A-series  (p - q = 1, \lambda  q = 48)

 q   \lambda    (a , b , c)            \Delta  = gcd(a , b , c)
 1   48  (144,  192,   240)      48
 2   24  (120,  288,   312)      24
 3   16  (112,  384,   400)      16
 4   12  (108,  480,   492)      12
 6    8  (104,  672,   680)       8
 8    6  (102,  864,   870)       6
12    4  (100, 1248,  1252)       4
16    3   ( 99, 1632,  1635)      3
24    2   ( 98, 2400,  2402)      2
48    1   ( 97, 4704,  4705)      1  \leftarrow  primitive

B-series  (p - q = 3, \lambda  q = 16)

 q   \lambda    (a , b , c)            \Delta  = gcd(a , b , c)
 1   16  (128,  240,   272)      16
 2    8  (160,  168,   232)       8
 4    4  (132,  224,   260)       4
 8    2  (114,  352,   370)       2
16    1  (105,  608,   617)       1  \leftarrow  primitive

We therefore obtain exactly 10 + 5 = 15 distinct integral right-angled triangles.

Step 6.  Primitivity  
Because gcd(p , q) = 1, equation (1) shows that the common divisor of x , y , z is precisely \lambda .  Consequently the triple is primitive iff \lambda  = 1.  Inspecting the tables we find precisely two such instances:

* (97, 4704, 4705) (from the A-series, q = 48),  
* (105, 608, 617) (from the B-series, q = 16).

Conclusion.  
Equation (\star ) possesses exactly fifteen integral solutions, listed above, and exactly two of the corresponding triangles are primitive, namely (97, 4704, 4705) and (105, 608, 617).

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
Compared with the original problem (“area = 2 × perimeter”) and the current kernel variant (“area = 8 × perimeter”), the enhanced variant raises the
difficulty in several independent ways.

1.  The multiplicative constant 24 gives the core Diophantine constraint
    λq(p–q)=48, whose factor structure (48 = 2⁴·3) is far richer than the
    previous constants 4 and 16.  It forces a systematic case
    decomposition that is impossible to guess by inspection.

2.  Because 48 has ten divisors and the odd-divisor condition admits two
    distinct gaps (p–q=1 and p–q=3), the solver must handle **two separate
    sub-families**, each still containing several possibilities.  This
    produces fifteen distinct triples instead of three or five, so mere
    “pattern spotting’’ or trial–and–error is hopeless; a rigorous factor
    analysis is unavoidable.

3.  Verifying primitivity is no longer immediate but requires an additional
    argument involving the exact role of the scaling parameter λ.

4.  The solution seamlessly blends several classical techniques
    (parametrisation of Pythagorean triples, parity arguments, coprimality
    considerations, divisor counting) which interact non-trivially.  Each
    step depends on previous observations, and skipping one of them breaks
    the entire chain.

5.  Finally, the task demands a complete *classification* (all 15 triples
    explicitly listed) and a *second* classification (primitive vs.
    non-primitive), greatly increasing both computational and conceptual
    workload.

original_kernel_variant.question: Prove that there are exactly fifteen right-angled triangles whose side-lengths are positive integers and whose area is numerically equal to twenty-four times their perimeter.  Moreover, decide which of these fifteen triples are primitive, i.e. whose three side-lengths are relatively prime.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Notation.

CASE B.  p - q = 3.

        q \in  {1, 2, 4, 8, 16},  \lambda  = 16 / q.                     (7)

Put p = q + 3; inserting in (1) yields

Step 5.  Explicit enumeration  
For each admissible q we compute (a_0 , b_0 , c) and finally write (a , b , c) with a \leq  b.

A-series  (p - q = 1, \lambda  q = 48)

 q   \lambda    (a , b , c)            \Delta  = gcd(a , b , c)
 1   48  (144,  192,   240)      48
 2   24  (120,  288,   312)      24
 3   16  (112,  384,   400)      16
 4   12  (108,  480,   492)      12
 6    8  (104,  672,   680)       8
 8    6  (102,  864,   870)       6
12    4  (100, 1248,  1252)       4
16    3   ( 99, 1632,  1635)      3
24    2   ( 98, 2400,  2402)      2
48    1   ( 97, 4704,  4705)      1  \leftarrow  primitive

B-series  (p - q = 3, \lambda  q = 16)

 q   \lambda    (a , b , c)            \Delta  = gcd(a , b , c)
 1   16  (128,  240,   272)      16
 2    8  (160,  168,   232)       8
 4    4  (132,  224,   260)       4
 8    2  (114,  352,   370)       2
16    1  (105,  608,   617)       1  \leftarrow  primitive

Step 6.  Primitivity
